---
title: "Book note: The Open Logic FOL texts"
date: "2026-06-12"
draft: true
---


 
Richard Zach has been the prime mover of the admirable Open Logic Project. The [project's website](https://openlogicproject.org) offers an "open-source, modular, collaboratively authored collection of teaching materials for formal (meta)logic and formal methods, starting at an intermediate level (i.e., after an introductory formal logic course). It is aimed at a non-mathematical audience (in particular, students of philosophy and computer science), but is completely rigorous."

There are two selections of chapters which cover core topics on First-Order Logic. One is "remixed" by Richard Zach under the title [*Sets, Logic, Computation: An Open Introduction to Metalogic*](https://slc.openlogicproject.org/slc-screen.pdf). The other is "remixed" by Michael Hallett and Richard Zach under the title [*Intermediate Logic: An Open Introduction*](https://builds.openlogicproject.org/courses/"s"-logic/il-screen.pdf). 

Part I in each case contains the same four chapters providing an introduction to informal "naive" set theory. This material was originally written by Tim Button (though as I will briefly point out in a moment, these remixes omit a couple of short passages which ought to matter to philosophers).

Part II of each remix again contains the same material, this time eight chapters on FOL with an added ninth chapter "Beyond First-order Logic". 

Then the two remixes diverge. Zach's original version adds a short two -chapter Part III, "Turing Machines". The Hallet and Zach remix instead has a long six-chapter Part III, "Incompleteness". I will return to discuss these divergent final parts in due course. For now I will very briefly consider Part I on set theory, and then concentrate on the coverage of FOL.

<hr>
